comment: Don't use a `std::runtime_error` -- we want to exclusively use our own exception types in this codebase. This is throwing for a pretty weird case that, theoretically, should never actually occur, so we don't need to define an exception type for it specifically. We can just use assertions. Something like this should suffice: ``` FC_ASSERT(block, "Transaction with ID ${tid} was indexed as being in block ID ${bid}, but no such block was found", ("tid", transaction_id")("bid", block_id)); FC_THROW("Transaction with ID ${tid} was indexed as being in block ID ${bid}, but was not found in that block", ("tid", transaction_id")("bid", block_id)); ```

comment: The expected number of unconfirmed blocks in a healthy network of 21 producers is around 360. With 1 MB blocks, that would mean we would need at least 360 MB for the unconfirmed database, and this line appears to put a hard ceiling of 128 MB. In an unhealthy network, the number of blocks required could easily exceed 360 and if the unconfirmed database size is too small, it will compound the problem by taking down more nodes (including potentially block producing nodes). I think this should be a separate configuration parameter like `cfg.shared_memory_size` and the default value should be at least 512 MB.

msg: The expected number of unconfirmed blocks in a healthy network of 21 producers is around 360. With 1 MB blocks, that would mean we would need at least 360 MB for the unconfirmed database, and this line appears to put a hard ceiling of 128 MB. In an unhealthy network, the number of blocks required could easily exceed 360 and if the unconfirmed database size is too small, it will compound the problem by taking down more nodes (including potentially block producing nodes). I think this should be a separate configuration parameter like `cfg.shared_memory_size` and the default value should be at least 512 MB.

comment: This isn't a really safe way to accomplish this. The producer plugin has several invariants it needs to maintain about the state of the block. At the very least this should call `schedule_production_loop` however, that will be heavyweight as it will re-apply all of the deferred transactions, unapplied transactions etc. As it is, before a transaction hits a speculative block it will be in a state where the time point is managed and the deferred/unapplied/persisted transactions are applied and after the first tx, you will have a clean block with a potentially different timestamp. This doesn't really present a consistent view of HEAD, nor would naively calling `schedule_production_loop` (which would apply some transactions). If we want to solve this in the producer plugin, we need to have a first class mode of operation and analyze all of the invariants the producer plugin holds for effects.
msg: This isn't a really safe way to accomplish this. The producer plugin has several invariants it needs to maintain about the state of the block. This doesn't really present a consistent view of HEAD, nor would naively calling `schedule_production_loop` (which would apply some transactions). If we want to solve this in the producer plugin, we need to have a first class mode of operation and analyze all of the invariants the producer plugin holds for effects.

comment: Our other black/whitelists are managed and maintained in the controller, this should probably go in the same place. Each `resource_limits_manager` method can take a single bool that indicates whether the operation in question should allow access or deny access to the elastic limits as opposed to now where it takes a flag which indicates the greylist may apply and then checks the greylist. That will lower the burden for future devs who will find all of the black/grey/whitelists in the same system and reduce the amount of "application" logic in the `resouce_limits_manager`

comment: My preference is for this assignment to only be done for locally-configured producers so that `_producer_watermarks` remains bounded in size no matter how long nodeos runs. So this line could be replaced with: ```cpp if( _producers.count( bsp->header.producer ) > 0 ) _producer_watermarks[bsp->header.producer] = bsp->block_num; ``` Or if you want to be slightly more efficient: ```cpp auto block_producer = bsp->header.producer; auto watermark_itr = _producer_watermarks.find( block_producer ); if( watermark_itr != _producer_watermarks.end() ) { watermark_itr->second = bsp->block_num; } else if( _producers.count( block_producer ) > 0 ) { watermark_itr.emplace( block_producer, bsp->block_num ); } ``` In fact, one could just replace `_producers` and `_producer_watermarks` with a single `std::unordered_map`, but that is a bigger change so let's not worry about that for this PR.
msg: My preference is for this assignment to only be done for locally-configured producers so that `_producer_watermarks` remains bounded in size no matter how long nodeos runs. So this line could be replaced with: ```cpp if( _producers.count( bsp->header.producer ) > 0 ) _producer_watermarks[bsp->header.producer] = bsp->block_num; ``` Or if you want to be slightly more efficient: ```cpp auto block_producer = bsp->header.producer; auto watermark_itr = _producer_watermarks.find( block_producer ); if( watermark_itr != _producer_watermarks.end() ) { watermark_itr->second = bsp->block_num; } else if( _producers.count( block_producer ) > 0 ) { watermark_itr.emplace( block_producer, bsp->block_num ); } ``` In fact, one could just replace `_producers` and `_producer_watermarks` with a single `std::unordered_map`, but that is a bigger change so let's not worry about that for this PR.

comment: Even though this complicates things, I really think `p2p-accept-transactions` belongs in the net_plugin and not here. I think what you could do is add another boolean (defaults to false) to the chain_plugin that tracks whether there is any source of transactions (this, or rather its negation, could be accessed via the `no_transactions` accessor). There would then need to be a function that other plugins could call which causes chain_plugin to consider possible other sources of transactions (and update that boolean accordingly). So if `api_accept_transactions` is true, `no_transactions` would always return false. But if `api_accept_transactions` is false, then `no_transactions` would return true by the end of the initialization stage of chain_plugin, but then later switch to false as other plugins (like the net_plugin) finish initialization.

comment: I think this belongs in the caller of `enable_deep_mind` in `chain_plugin.cpp`. This is setting things up for the logger, so whatever component owns and manages that (`chain_plugin` in this case) should be the one to call it once after it decides it is going to enable Deep Mind logging within nodeos. This call to `controller::enable_deep_mind` is just telling `controller` to use the already established and properly setup logger; it shouldn't be responsible to do further setup.
msg: I think this belongs in the caller of `enable_deep_mind` in `chain_plugin.cpp`. +/**
+   It was reported from a customer that when file system which 
+   "data/blocks" belongs to is running out of space, the producer

+   "corrupted" in that it no longer contained all the irreversible blocks.
+   It was also observed that when file system which "data/state"
+   belons to is running out of space, nodeos will crash with SIGBUS as
+   the state file is unable to acquire new pages.
+
+   The solution is to have a dedicated plugin to monitor resource 
+   usages (file system space now, CPU, memory, and networking
+   bandwidth in the future).
+   The plugin uses a thread to periodically check space usage of file
+   systems of directories being monitored. If space used
+   is over a predefined threshold, a graceful shutdown is initiated.
+
+   The directories to be monitored are configured;
+   they can be any combination of data, data/blocks, data/state and
+   any directories like those for trace and state history.
+   Space threshold can also be configured.
+
+   Alternative solution to blocks directory space exhaustion
+   was considered: starting from block_log::append, 
+   make sure all exceptions was handled properly. But it was deemed to be
+   too tedious and error prone to review all possible excpetions, and
+   might likely break concensus. The plugin approach is more generic,
+   monitoring any file systems and without modifying existing code.
+
+**/
+

+#include <eosio/chain/exceptions.hpp>
